text: What is self-attention? A mechanism where each token attends to all other tokens in the same sequence to capture relationships.
Why self-attention is important? It allows the model to understand context and dependencies between words regardless of distance.
What problem does self-attention solve? It handles long-range dependencies better than RNNs.
How does self-attention work? It computes attention scores between tokens using query, key, and value vectors.
What is output of self-attention? A weighted combination of value vectors based on attention scores.
What is multi-head attention? Multiple attention mechanisms running in parallel to capture different relationships.
Why multi-head attention used? It helps the model learn different types of patterns simultaneously.
How multi-head attention works? Input is split into multiple heads, each computes attention separately, then combined.
What is benefit of multiple heads? Each head focuses on different semantic aspects of the input.
What is final output? Concatenation of all head outputs followed by linear transformation.
What is query (Q)? Representation of the current token seeking relevant information.
What is key (K)? Representation of tokens used to match against query.
What is value (V)? Contains actual information passed after attention.
Why Q, K, V needed? They allow flexible computation of attention scores.
How Q, K, V generated? Through linear transformations of input embeddings.
What is scaled dot-product attention? Computes attention using QK^T divided by sqrt(d).
Why scaling used? Prevents large values that can destabilize softmax.
What is output? Softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d)) multiplied by V.
Why softmax used? Converts scores into probability distribution.
What does attention score represent? Importance of one token relative to another.
What is positional encoding? Technique to add order information to token embeddings.
Why positional encoding needed? Transformers process tokens in parallel and lack sequence awareness.
What is sinusoidal encoding? Uses sine and cosine functions to represent positions.
What is learned positional encoding? Position embeddings learned during training.
What is benefit? Helps model understand word order in sentences.
What is masked attention? Prevents model from seeing future tokens during training.
Why masking used? Ensures autoregressive behavior in text generation.
Where used? In decoder part of transformer.
What problem solved? Prevents information leakage from future tokens.
How implemented? Using a mask matrix applied before softmax.
What is encoder? Part of transformer that processes input sequence into meaningful representations.
What does encoder contain? Self-attention and feedforward layers.
What is encoder output? Context-rich representations of input tokens.
Where encoder used? In models like BERT.
What is purpose? Understand input context deeply.
What is decoder? Part of transformer that generates output sequence.
What layers in decoder? Masked attention, encoder-decoder attention, feedforward.
What is encoder-decoder attention? Decoder attends to encoder output.
Where decoder used? In GPT and translation models.
What is purpose? Generate tokens sequentially.
What is feedforward layer? Fully connected neural network applied after attention.
Why needed? Adds non-linearity and transformation power.
How applied? Independently to each token.
What is structure? Two linear layers with activation function.
What is benefit? Enhances representation learning.
What is residual connection? Adds input to output of a layer.
Why used? Prevents vanishing gradients.
What is skip connection? Another name for residual connection.
Where applied? After attention and feedforward layers.
What is benefit? Improves deep network training.
What is layer normalization? Normalizes inputs across features.
Why used? Stabilizes and speeds up training.
Where applied? After each sub-layer.
Difference from batch norm? Works per sample, not batch.
What is benefit? Improves convergence.
